title: Raising awareness of the racial disparities in the impact of colorectal cancer on the Black community.
plain_summary: This bill recognizes that Black Americans are more likely to get colorectal cancer and die from it compared to white Americans. The bill wants more research to find out why this difference exists. It also encourages better cancer screening programs that focus on helping Black Americans and younger people who might be at higher risk. The goal is to make sure everyone has a fair chance to catch and treat colorectal cancer early.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce.

sections.title: Resolution Title
sections.text: This resolution is about raising awareness of how colorectal cancer affects Black Americans more than other racial groups.

sections.title: Background Facts
sections.text: Colorectal cancer (also called CRC) is cancer that starts in the colon or rectum. It is the third most common cancer in the United States for both men and women together. It is also the second biggest cause of cancer deaths. This disease kills more than fifty thousand people each year. Black men get colorectal cancer twenty percent more often than white men who are not Hispanic. Black women get it fourteen percent more often than white women who are not Hispanic. Black men die from colorectal cancer forty percent more often than white men who are not Hispanic. Black women die from it twenty-five percent more often than white women who are not Hispanic. Colorectal cancer is the third biggest cause of cancer deaths in both Black men and women in the United States. Black Americans have the lowest five-year survival rate for colorectal cancer of any racial group in the United States. Deaths from colorectal cancer among people younger than fifty-five have gone up by almost two percent each year from two thousand eleven to two thousand nineteen. The death of actor Chadwick Boseman from colorectal cancer when he was forty-three years old has made more people talk about how this cancer affects the Black community. By two thousand thirty, experts think colorectal cancer will be the leading cause of death among people ages twenty to forty-nine. About nineteen percent of the difference in death rates between white and Black Americans comes from Black Americans getting screened less often. When colorectal cancer is found at the earliest stage, ninety percent of people survive. There are many ways to screen for this cancer.

sections.title: House of Representatives Resolution
sections.text: The House of Representatives agrees to do these things. First, it recognizes that colorectal cancer has a deadly impact on the American people. Second, it acknowledges that the Black community faces unfair differences when dealing with colorectal cancer. Third, it encourages the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (a government health agency) to continue and expand their work to find out what causes the differences in screening rates between racial groups. It also wants them to develop good ways to reduce and eventually eliminate these unfair differences in colorectal screening. Fourth, it encourages everyone to get colorectal cancer screening when the United States Preventive Services Task Force (a group of medical experts) recommends it. Fifth, it encourages the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention to do research to find any environmental factors that might cause colorectal cancer in young adults. It also wants the National Institutes of Health (a government research agency) to study any body-related factors that cause higher risk for colorectal cancer in young adults. Sixth, it urges state health plans to quickly adopt new rules to cover colorectal screenings for people at a younger age. It wants special consideration for the Black community and all people who have higher risk for colorectal cancer.
